speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of Fred Jackson, on Monday July 25, 1994, in Beverly Hills, California. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Do you remember where we were? I mean, you remember where you were when-- what you were telling me. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a civilian train, and there were uniformed nurses on it-- all German nurses you could see. And it was-- they bring-- the train came in from Holland. And they understood. I didn't count. It was 300 Jewish kids in it, from infancy to about four years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, you know, I told them, I'm sure they going to put them in the gas chamber. But this is not-- did not happen with this transport. I did not see it, but that's what I understood from people who did, in Auschwitz. They dug a big hole. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put in water and gasoline. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They lit them up. [CRYING] And they burned them alive. Because we could hear the screaming, the-- you know what I mean-- the terrible pain. But this I did not witness myself. But I was sure they go to the gas chamber, you know, I wasn't-- I knew they not going to live, you know what I mean. I never seen a civilian train coming to-- you know, normal, you know, not cattle cart. And I realized that a 1/2 an hour later, the train pulled out with the nurses-- you know what I mean-- that I seen. And this was one of the terrible things-- which I don't have too many nightmares but this one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's terrible. After you have children and grandchildren, you realize it yourself what a horrible nightmare was it-- to burning somebody alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- then I asked one of the kapo, why did they burn them alive? He said, there was no room in the crematorium that day. Too many transports came in. And they didn't want to be bothered with the cries and screams, which, you know, with those infidels. So they burned them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I know that when we were marching from the lager, that's in Kochendorf, towards the mine-- there was 100 in our group-- and every time went out, the SS who counted, you know, at the gate, he said laughingly, I hope you don't bring back too many of them. We don't need them. New transport is coming. That was the tragedy-- always new people are coming. They made room. And they were fresher, you know. We were already worn out. And SS said, leave it to me. Don't worry about it. Just relax. We make enough room for you. I understood very well German, so I knew exactly what he was saying. Because I did took the German language in the school. That was compulsory. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know, this one done to us. We never hurt anybody. My parents were hardworking people. They made it. They were very successful. They employed over 100 people. They were good to the help. They were good to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what sort of business your father had and what life was like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father has a building material business-- mostly heavy industry. Like, he built the roads, you know, asphalt roads, you know, between one city to the other one, from Uzhhorod to [NON-ENGLISH] I remember a big by-- and from Uzhhorod to [NON-ENGLISH], and all those, you know, 10 miles, 15 miles. We had big machinery. My father was also a builder of big buildings, not-- commercial buildings. Like he had, you know-- there was a parliament in Uzhhorod for seven story high. He was the major builder. There were three bridges in Uzhhorod and over the river. The last one, the new one, he built it. I don't mean he. I mean his company built it. Otherwise, he was in there heavy. He was very, very big turn with the Czech government, mostly because they were all governmental jobs. And he was very successful. Nobody did-- [CRYING] Nobody can ask for a better father. I hope I'd be half of-- as good-- [CRYING] --as he was. He lived for the children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His dream was when the boys grow up, everyone have a different-- like he always wanted me to be a banker, because I was so good in math. I was outstanding always in math. That was my favorite subject. He always said, you be the banker. He already figured it out-- provided us with everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was an angel. [CRYING] They really did their best to help us, to improve us, to make better, easier life. Because he was working very hard. He was in that business, but although the business was opened only five and a half days-- Saturday noon and no Saturday/ Sunday. But whenever he was Sunday at home, and said all he done is just breaking his head. The books, you know, he did the books and everything then. He brought more ways in. And he taught me already at the age of 10, how to do things and helped me a life-- helped me a lot in life-- what I learned from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family life like in Uzhhorod? And tell me about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent-- very good life. And like I told you, they were well-to-do. We had everything we needed, you know what I mean? We went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- what was the Jewish life like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life was, yeah. I belonged to the organization, Zionist-- Kadima. I belonged there, and we went every summer to a-- like boy scout camp, you know, with the-- kind-- we had a uniform, the kind of uniform, you know. And we went to boy scout camp. And I was there about for six weeks every year, you know, summer, when school was out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was-- my family was strictly orthodox, you know what I mean? And they retained a real kosher house. My father told me-- and he traveled a lot, because he had to go to Prague, to Budapest, to Vienna, and all over, you know, for business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only thing I ate is eggs and toast. That's what he told me. Coffee, naturally, I drink in moderation, because I don't eat what's not kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I went to somewhere-- and-- but he was a moderate Jew. You know, my grandfather had beard from here to here-- my grandfather, you know what mean? But he was only moderate, you know what I mean-- no facial hair or anything, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- well taken children, you know what I mean-- well brought up-- very little problem. We didn't cause no problem-- and just a normal, good life, a normal good life. There was no reason for any type-- he was not political. His political affiliation-- was he was a Social Democrat. In Czechoslovakia, there were two major parties, actually. There were many small ones-- I think four or five-- were Agrarian and so forth. There were Social Democrats and National Democrats. Most Jewish people belonged to the middle. That was that was a middle of the road party-- Social Democrat-- you know what I mean-- which was similar here to the Democratic, even the Republican Party. Because it was not-- it wasn't a real liberal. It wasn't real conservative either. It was like a moderate Republican and conservative-- you know what I mean-- Democrats.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived a normal life until 1938. Let me tell you something very interesting. Some people who were in Poland-- and then Poland was invaded first. And they came there-- Jews. And they was telling us these horror stories. And this is the tragedy of our life. We never believed them. What sort of stories were they telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were telling they were-- they didn't tell-- there was no concentration camp. There was no-- there was shooting. They said that they take other Jews from each shtetl villages, and they just never come back. And one told us-- I remember, because when he came over-- he ran away. He stayed in our home for a couple days. And he said that there were mass shooting and that he escaped, because he had typhus. And he was in the hospital. And when he was supposed to go back, he just ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all said, the poor guy. He had typhus, you know what I mean? He's exaggerating. You know. It was a tragedy, but we Jews could not believe that in the 20th century this type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: --terrible Holocaust and-- caused for no reason whatsoever, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I even understand when they-- I seen in Uzhhorod public hanging-- five Jewish guys. They were Communist. It's true. They were Communists, and they hanged them-- public hanging. But they hanged them because they were Communists. But the hanged Communists, if they were not Jewish either. So we cannot, in that perspective, you cannot just single out because they were Jews. No. They hanged them because they were Communists. But they hanged also non-Jews Communists, which I did not believe in anyway. For the-- any-- I don't believe in anybody for political difference should be hanged, or jailed, or any type of this one. But to take the children and the whole families for no reason from one day to the other one, and just load them in cattle cars, and massacre them, kill them with all this cruelty, this went beyond my imagination. I never.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an extremely smart man. I talked to him in the cattle car sitting down. I told him, Daddy, what's going to happen-- I told him-- after all, you know? And he always assured me. Don't worry. This is the 20th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] They're not going to kill us, like I told you. And thank God, in a way, that we believed these things. Because if we'd believe, there would be a panic and killing each other in mass proportion.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Polish man came-- the Polish man who came to your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened from the time he came until you were put in the ghetto? What was your life like while the war was going on, but yet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this one was in early.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your life like from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: My life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No problem. The Hungarian occupied the-- you know, Czechoslovakia was divided into the Protectorate-- Moravia and Bohemia. Slovakia became independent. And Podkarpatska Rus, or the-- under Carpathian-- where we lived-- that became Hungarian. My mother was Hungarian. My father-- and we had about six or seven generation go, but they all were born in Uzhhorod. My grandfather, great-- and you know-- all were born in Uzhhorod. You know, they were all slowly, slowly building up. When my father reached the pinnacle on his 30 year, he was a big man already-- but tremendous work, hard work, and honest work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between-- since the Hungarian occupation, the young ones went to labor camp-- you know, between 18 and 20-- all to the labor camp. An under 18, they stayed home. The families were not harmed, OK? The families that were not born in Uzhhorod-- Polish, and quite a bit families-- they were taken to Poland. So they were not citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have all the freedom we wanted, naturally. And as year went by it became always worse and worse. School was very segregated, you know, very segregated. They called it a numerous losses-- out of about 6 out of 100-- but it was not true. They took maybe 1 out of 500. You applied. It was advantage of going to these privileged schools, because then you didn't have to go to labor camp. The Hungarians inspected a school. You didn't have to labor camp. And I was, like I told you, until the German came in, which was about in February 1945-- February or March-- I don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --excuse me, '44, of course. You know, there was no family-style-- that's it-- you know, I mean, not like animals. The Hungarians were not our friend, but somehow they tolerated us. Because they benefited on us. They knew that until then the Jewish industry and the Jewish business, they are going to survive too. I can tell you a cute story. My friend, Justin Betlev, a couple months ago, from Budapest-- he went there. And he told me he was on the train-- not on the train-- you know, which goes in the city-- like an underground--

speaker: interviewer
text: The subway.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- yeah. He was there on the train, and he heard two Hungarian men talking. He understands Hungarian. He says, you know, the fruit is very expensive, you know? And other lady said, well, only the Jews can afford it. That was now-- two months ago, right? Only the Jews can afford it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he spoke up. He says, well, you see what happens when you chase other Jews, then you suffer like this. He says-- he said it to them. You see, you got rid of the Jews, now you suffer like this. They just moved away, you know what I mean. They didn't know. And they said, American, American, American, you know what I mean? But he basically was born in the USA. But he had some grandparents over there, and he went to visit their grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when once the Germans came in, everything changed overnight-- immediately, loudspeakers and posters all over. Jews cannot work as doctors. Jews cannot work as-- you know, even those doctors they took care of only Jews. Everybody who had office job, you know what I mean-- government job, is out. No banks-- Jews cannot open-- cannot own the businesses, like my business-- like not mine-- my father's business. One of our help, who wasn't Jewish-- he was OK-- he didn't do-- turn it over the whole business to him. Everything was-- all of a sudden, it wasn't on my father's name. It was on his name everything. And we just struggled for about a month. After the month, approximately, they took us to the ghetto. And ghetto life was miserable. I don't have to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do tell me. You were in the ghetto for how long and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ghetto about four weeks-- if I have to guess. I don't really recall exactly, you know what I mean, because it's impossible. That was 50-- 51 years ago. And-- but in the ghetto, well, we had a corner-- all right, a corner-- I would say 3 by 5, OK? We slept on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about we, who are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about my father-- my father, my mother, my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sister, no. Sister was-- did not live in Uzhhorod, because my sister married. And he lived in a city, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was taken from there to Auschwitz. We find that she died. She had a little boy of five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, naturally, if you have a boy of five, you went immediately to the right, you know. That was the Mengele system, you know. Children, everybody went to the right. He-- she did not want it. She was married, you know. She was married about 300, 400 miles from us. The minute they closed, nobody could move anywhere. So you were telling us about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in the ghetto, right? You know, the fear-- people started being very skeptical. You know, the fear snuck in. But we still-- we figured, as long as we're get-- and we knew the war is coming to the end. It was already-- you know what I mean-- '44. And we used to listen-- before they took us to the camp-- to the BBC. We had a good radio, you know. And we got BBC. And they said the invasion-- you know-- not the invasion-- no. Excuse me, the invasion wasn't yet-- the bombing, you know, was tremendous. And they bomb all the German cities. And the war is soon-- they said, maybe three months, maybe six months, you know. So they gave us hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: But bother me more in my life than anything else, that I was in Auschwitz when there was an air raid-- American planes, which was visible in the sky, OK? And they bombed the city of Auschwitz, not a camp-- the city of Auschwitz about three miles, two miles from us. You know there was industry there, and they never bothered to bomb the railways.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This I never will understand. And this is beyond my comprehension why this thing happened, you know what I mean? Because it was there. It's a minute away. All they had to do is bomb the railways and they would stop transportation. A lot of people, hundreds of thousands of people, could have been saved, but they didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had these questions. I was interviewed once here too. And I asked him. And he said, well, he heard that Roosevelt said-- which I don't know if it's true or not-- that it wasn't the first priority. It was the industry which was the first priority. You know, Roosevelt was-- always thought the best friend of the Jews. That's the biggest mistake anybody thinks, because when he sandbagged those kids-- you know that boat, which came-- to sure death-- in my eyes, that person doesn't exist for me. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we-- could we go back to-- you were describing what-- what it was like for you or your parents, and-- and your brothers in the ghetto? What-- you were saying it was 3 by 5. Could you go back to that and describe for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was public toilets and public facilities. That's where I went. It was a-- about 3 by 5. And we-- we slept-- we slept there. And we stayed there. We could walk in the ghetto, yes. We could go and walk, but when we-- but we all went. We never left the space alone. Because we knew if we leave it, somebody else going to come in, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was packed. There was about-- I don't know how many-- 10,000, 20,000 people-- you know what I mean-- in a place where-- 1 square mile. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food-- as far as I remember-- we did not receive food there. You-- you brought in food. And then you can buy and sell. I don't really-- it's funny that I don't remember that-- what was-- how we received the food in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have any dealings yourself on the black market at that point in your life, or were you dealing underground or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. There was no black market-- not-- not as far as I knew. Maybe there was. I'm sure it was, but I didn't know about it. No. We didn't have to buy it on the black market.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the ghetto itself, they brought in food which-- that's right. That was the black market. Exactly. You're right. I mean, I wasn't involved in it. And you could buy for money. All right, it wasn't even money. You had a gold ring, you had any type of jewelry, you know. Or if you had-- then you could buy it, you know what I mean? I know one thing-- that my father came to me. And he told me-- and that must have cost him an awful lot of money-- not that it matters now, because everyone was taking away everything-- that he got a visa for me to go to Switzerland. But he could get a visa only for me, not for my other brothers-- not because of me, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is true I was my father's favorite. That's true. My brother, who is alive, who went to Israel, he was my mother's favorite. But that's how it was, you know, family. But he says-- and I-- no. I tell him, no way. If not everybody goes, I stay. And I want to tell you something. All that changed my life. And I look hindsight at it, which I could not help him or anything. I'm still happy I didn't, because your imagination, your fantasy runs away. Maybe you could have done something. Because to talk about a concentration camp, the only way you can do it-- if you went through it. No other person can describe you what really-- really it honestly was unless you were in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And ghetto was very short, only a few weeks, you know, very-- only thing you did, at night you lay down-- two days-- you know. And during the day, we're walking around, go to visit one neighbor or other in the ghetto, naturally-- visit. You went 100 yards, there was another family. You know what I mean? There-- there were a lot of people-- prominent people-- who were taken out from the ghetto-- closest here from the SS headquarter-- and they were tortured terribly. Where is their money? Where is this? You know what I mean. Your political affiliation, who you know, do you know any underground thing, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were taken out, and they were sent back. And you could see how they looked, you know. They all beaten up, you know. The face was distorted, their teeth knocked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a very good friend of my father who was a lawyer, Dr. Miklos. He was taken out. And he came back. And he right away came to my father and said, if they take you out, Joe-- my father's name was Joe-- take you out, don't even-- tell them everything they want to hear and lie more. Tell them here and here, you know? Because everybody, like us too, had hidden stuff, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we knew already they not take it away, so made a false wall in the basement. And that's where hidden stuff were. We hope when we come back, whoever going to come back going to find it. We didn't find nothing. That's-- [LAUGHS] we didn't even-- but who cares. That was a second thought. But as far as death, you know, in the ghetto, except those exceptions-- and political mostly-- but taken out, you know what I mean, it was nothing, really. It was sure fire, because don't forget that the-- we were not four weeks after the ghetto. But they started taking out right away, immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Hungarian's gendarme, the other one-- they handled it. They were just too busy with the movement of the transport than to worry about us who were stayed. Every day they came out, and they had-- the-- there was a Jewish community in the ghetto who had to give them the list of every-- and every single person and child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans decided how they take it. And as they took them out, it was less and less people, you know, because they was worse now. And eventually, we went out with my parents and the other one-- and with the last transport. Because for the simple reason, my father was in the building business. And he brought in water through a pipe from the main to the camp to have water, to wash yourself, and water to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: So obviously, the German felt he is important enough to leave him to the last. And so we had to be the last transportation. So that was the life. There wasn't-- it was fear. But nobody believed the ultimate. That's why-- you understand what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, you are worried about it, but nobody believed they gonna kill us, you know what I mean-- in that sense. If-- we-- I figured that I'm not old to work. And I'm going to work hard, whatever they tell me-- labor, slave labor, heavy-- but my parents going to be left alone. And that gave me enough satisfaction that I was willing to do it. But unfortunately, it did not turn out to be that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were talking about Kochendorf--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were-- we-- we have sort of circumvented and moved around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we can-- if we could go back to Kochendorf, and you could tell me from that point on--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what your life was like and what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The life in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Do you want to switch tapes, or do you want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's up to you. You got two minutes. You can speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have got two minutes on this-- left on this tape. I think maybe you want to switch the tape? I think it's better than to stop you in the middle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I go to the bathroom for one second?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.
